title: Nietszche aspect 2
wikitext:
[]: Nietszche aspect 2             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Tue Nov 20, 2007 1:53 pm Alright, this is the continuation. NO BLOODY MESSING AROUND HERE!

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Tue Nov 20, 2007 7:45 pm hehe, sorry about that last time.

[]:             Quote Post by JP » Tue Nov 20, 2007 8:12 pm Oh lawd, is that sum Nietszche?

[]:             Quote Post by BrikHaus » Tue Nov 20, 2007 8:36 pm Why don't you combine Nietszche, Wagner, and Cadets into one giant orgy thread?

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Wed Nov 21, 2007 6:16 am Not th ecadet thread. It won't make sense. Nietzsche's ideas were reflected by Wagner, and vice versa, so we find many correlating things. That should have been explained quite a lot, but never mind.

[]:             Quote Post by slothen » Thu Nov 22, 2007 5:05 am I would contribute, but my knowledge of the aforementioned is rather limited.

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Thu Nov 22, 2007 7:19 am All this reminds me of the Isaac Asimov story where a physicist brings back Shakespeare, who storms out disgusted when he sees all the crazy, stupid things people have been digging out of his plays that he didn't put there. No, I'm not comparing Anno to Shakespeare , but don't you think all this talk about Nietsche (Which, like Slothen says, few of us know about) is digging too deep into Evangelion?

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Thu Nov 22, 2007 5:48 pm “If you desire peace of soul and happiness, then believe; if you would be disciple of truth, then inquire”-Nietzsche (obviously). Anno is forcing this on us, and there is nothing too ‘deep’. We must inquire, and from my past 4 years of meticulous dissection (no jokes on this please) of Eva, 3 things stand out: Nietzsche, Wagner, and Anno’s form of a political protest. Of course, there are things that is a lot more than this, like technical points moral points etc. but all the psychological, and moral bits, all stem from Nietzsche (and Schopenhauer behind it + some Freud and Jung), and the political protest and the interaction of characters, and the eventual doom resembles Wagner a lot. Also, chance of a new beginning after total destruction? Wagner. Back on Nietzsche, the Will to Power and the Superman is shown by Shinji and Asuka in EoE: by abandoning the rest of humanity (or what is left of it), mostly out of disgust, but they allowed themselves to come on top, not completely giving into the ‘herd’, but to come out of the ‘herd’ to process the new beginning of mankind.

[]:             Quote Post by V » Thu Nov 22, 2007 6:17 pm Man killed god and was left with a blank slate to write his own future and own morality on -- that's about as much of a Nietzsche connection there is. I'd argue more for psychological interpretations like Jung and Freud, etc. much more. I agree with what UrsosArctos said about eventually running out of stuff and just digging things that are not really there. No, I do not think it is *Particularly* a political commentary. Indeed, I feel it has more to do with Mill and Rousseau's bohemian solitary walker. But I digress....

[]:             Quote Post by slothen » Thu Nov 22, 2007 6:37 pm The idea of a new beginning coming from total destruction is not something unique to Wagner.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Thu Nov 22, 2007 7:01 pm It's a common religious zealot trope infact. Who fits that bill perfectly in NGE?

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Fri Nov 23, 2007 3:26 am Yes, but Wagner made this absolutely clear, and with his connection to Nietzsche...?

[]:             Quote Post by Zuggy » Fri Nov 23, 2007 8:02 am C&P from last thread - God, I tried to read Thus Spake Zarathustra ages ago and got nowhere. I had no idea what he was getting at unless the language was really plain about it. I'm not seeing where the idea of Shinji and Asuka coming out of the LCL sea has to do with their will to power, didn't they both pop up on that beach out of their own despair rather than willing themselves to live and conquer the new world? Remember in 25/26 folks say of Shinji that he just wanted "death, sorrow, and to return to nothingness" and the only way he could do that was by ejecting himself from complementation (I guess the same applies to Asuka too).

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Sat Nov 24, 2007 2:23 pm Their despair was there way of making themselves free. It doesn't matter, they still willed themselves to be free (even for the worst reasons), and that made them to the Superman (and woman) Nietzsche 'predicted'.

[]:             Quote Post by Hunter21 » Sat Nov 24, 2007 3:19 pm Here is a question for you, Imperialist. If you understand Nietzsche so well, tell us the roles and views of the man when pertaining to woman. Then explain how the female characters in Evangelion fit in with those beliefs. And once you are done with that explain why Anno makes females as strong characters in many of his other anime (Gunbuster, Nadia, re:Cutie Honey, etc...) if he truly was influenced by the backwater philosopher.

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Sat Nov 24, 2007 3:33 pm Nietzsche describes woman as supposedly intelligent, and general praises (except for the part where Nietzsche describes woman as pretty hysterical). He also says that there must be a difference made wider between mem and women in general. He also hints that women's main job is to be pregnant-"Is there a more sacred state than pregnancy?". From these, I think that men and women should be roughly equal (he actually criticizes men that they are childish, but women understands children the best) Now for your second question, isn't it obvious? There are some points like Asuka still doesn't understand Shinji, they both have similarities, but their charcters as their sex is pretty much like oil and water, and the "pregnancy is a sacred state" I believe, was subtley put in when Asuka was having her periods and the relevant quote from her (she was saying she didn't want children, but there is still a reverse psychology here for us. I haven't watched other Anno works except for a few Studio Gibri works he collaborated, but doesn't the stong woman character fit with Nietzsche's ideals?

[]:             Quote Post by Ornette » Sat Nov 24, 2007 3:55 pm The Imperialist wrote: He also hints that women's main job is to be pregnant

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Sat Nov 24, 2007 3:58 pm Oh, sorry, not those two, but I meant the fact that women are intelligent, are dangerous and a diversion (the 2 things men want, oh wait, I didn't mantion this- oh well, now I did) and they have more responsbility than men (men are more childish).

[]:             Quote Post by Hunter21 » Sat Nov 24, 2007 3:59 pm The Imperialist wrote: Deleted the top stuff as you obviously pulled it out of your ass and not his teachings.

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Sat Nov 24, 2007 4:12 pm He does praise them. Also, I told you, I am not too sure about your third question, and when you mean strong, you mean as if in physical terms, I wasn't, and I do admit, Nietzsche say that women are unstable. Also, I remember saying that in On Wager, some ideas are then contradicted by Anno. I still do stick to that point that women are supposed to be intelligent. (It is unfeminine for a women to be unintelligent)
